justification: All pass criteria met: (1) 4 bounded contexts clearly identified and named (Route Planning, Delivery Fulfillment, Fleet & Driver Management, Customer Notification). (2) Cross-context dependencies explicitly called out — vehicle class from Fleet Management constraining Route Planning (RP-3 references FM-1), delivery failure in Fulfillment triggering Route recalculation (RP-2/DF-2), route position changes triggering Customer Notification (CN-1). (3) Invariant classification correct — vehicle class constraint FM-1 marked invariant true, stop time RP-4 marked 'No (soft)'. (4) 6 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria in the core path alone, plus 9 edge case scenarios. (5) Route recalculation triggers correctly enumerated as exactly three: new delivery added, delivery marked failed, driver deviation.

observations.text: Exceptional bounded context separation — identified all 4 contexts with clear responsibility boundaries and a well-structured table
Cross-context dependencies are not just mentioned but formally analyzed with rule cross-references (e.g., RP-3 explicitly references FM-1 vehicle class constraint)
Invariant classification is precise — vehicle class (true), route recalculation triggers (true), stop time (false), notification window threshold (implicitly configurable via 'next-3-stops')
Rich ubiquitous language glossary with 12 precisely defined terms — goes well beyond minimum requirements
Edge cases are exceptionally thorough — covers recalculation storms/debouncing, mid-route shift end, zero-minute stop time floor, route capacity limits
Rejection reasons section explicitly calls out the vehicle class UI-only enforcement anti-pattern — directly addresses the regulatory nature of the constraint
Business impact analysis includes quantified estimates (10% efficiency → 15-20% cost reduction) and identifies recalculation cascade risk
CN-2 (delay notification when bumped from next-3-stops window) is a sophisticated rule that many analyses would miss

Cross-context dependencies could be more formally structured as explicit integration points for MJ — they are spread across rules rather than consolidated into a dedicated 'Integration Points' section
Confidence assessment is honest at 78% with specific low-confidence areas (deviation threshold definition, debounce policy, route capacity) that correctly identify spec gaps

## Domain Analysis

### Business Context

This feature encodes the **dynamic delivery dispatch and route planning** process — a core operational capability for any logistics platform. It sits at the intersection of three major business concerns: **workforce management** (driver shifts, licensing), **delivery fulfillment** (pending deliveries, failure handling), and **customer experience** (proactive notifications).
